mnemonics: Jordan Blocks are like Diagonal's Sloppy Cousins: They're almost diagonal but have 1s on the superdiagonal when eigenvalues are defective.
keyPoints: The Jordan Canonical Form is a block diagonal matrix where each block (Jordan block) corresponds to an eigenvalue.
The size of a Jordan block is determined by the length of the chain of generalized eigenvectors for that eigenvalue.
It provides the best possible representation of a linear transformation when the matrix is not diagonalizable.
commonMistakes: Assuming the algebraic multiplicity equals the geometric multiplicity for all matrices (this is only true for diagonalizable matrices).
Forgetting that the 1s in a Jordan block are always on the superdiagonal (the line immediately above the main diagonal).
Mistaking the order of Jordan blocks; they can appear in any order along the diagonal, but the structure for each eigenvalue is unique.
